metadata.summary: Design and provision Azure Virtual Networks with hub-spoke topology: address space planning, subnet sizing, VNet peering, and Bastion for secure access.

content.title: Azure VNet and Hub-Spoke Topology
content.summary: Foundation of Azure networking: Virtual Network (VNet) provides network isolation with subnets, peering, and delegations. Hub-spoke topology centralises shared services (Firewall, Bastion, DNS) in a hub VNet while workloads live in spoke VNets connected via peering.

why.p: Azure VNet is the mandatory isolation boundary for all compute and PaaS resources. Without a deliberate CIDR plan, overlapping address spaces block VNet peering or on-premises VPN connectivity — issues that require full re-IP and re-deploy to fix. Hub-spoke separates shared networking infrastructure from workload VNets, enabling independent lifecycle management and consistent policy enforcement without duplicating Firewalls or Bastion hosts per spoke.

VNet peering does NOT transit: if spoke A and spoke B both peer with the hub, A and B cannot reach each other through the hub unless a UDR (User Defined Route) forces traffic through Azure Firewall in the hub. Agents routinely draw the topology diagram and forget this.

list.item: Designing landing-zone networks for new Azure subscriptions with multiple workloads or environments.
Multi-team environments where workloads share Firewall, Bastion, DNS, or VPN Gateway.
Compliance scenarios (HIPAA, PCI-DSS) requiring strict east-west traffic inspection between VNets.
Any setup with on-premises connectivity via VPN Gateway or ExpressRoute — the Gateway lives in the hub.
Single-region, single-VNet, single-team workload that does not cross subscriptions — full hub-spoke is overkill, use a flat VNet.
Cost-sensitive POCs — hub-spoke with Firewall + Bastion combined runs hundreds USD/month idle.
Throwaway dev environments — a flat VNet with NSG is sufficient.

section.name: address-space-planning
section.p: Plan address space before any resource is created. Overlapping CIDRs permanently block peering and VPN connectivity.

rule.statement: Use /16 for each VNet (hub and per-spoke). Use /24 for each subnet. Reserve /22 per spoke for AKS pod CIDR (AKS needs a large contiguous block for pods — typically /16 within the cluster but the node subnet must be sized accordingly). Leave at least 50% headroom in each VNet for growth.
rule.rationale: Azure reserves 5 IPs per subnet. AKS with kubenet or Azure CNI requires large pod CIDRs that overflow small subnets. Planning /24 subnets avoids the common /28 or /27 that runs out immediately when adding NAT Gateway, App Gateway, and Bastion reserved IPs.

rule.statement: Hub VNet MUST NOT overlap with any spoke VNet or on-premises range. Spoke VNets MUST NOT overlap with each other. Use a CIDR allocation table (Markdown or spreadsheet) as the source of truth before writing any Terraform.
rule.rationale: Peering fails immediately if CIDRs overlap. On-premises VPN/ER routes are also rejected. There is no in-place re-IP; you must delete and recreate.

example.title: Non-overlapping hub-spoke CIDR plan
example.code:
Hub VNet: 10.0.0.0/16 GatewaySubnet: 10.0.0.0/24 AzureFirewallSubnet: 10.0.1.0/24 AzureBastionSubnet: 10.0.2.0/24 shared-services: 10.0.3.0/24
Spoke prod: 10.1.0.0/16 appgw: 10.1.0.0/24 web: 10.1.1.0/24 app: 10.1.2.0/24 data: 10.1.3.0/24 private-endpoints: 10.1.4.0/24
Spoke dev: 10.2.0.0/16 (same subnet layout, different range)

section.name: subnet-delegations
section.p: Certain Azure services require subnet delegation — they claim exclusive use of the subnet.

rule.statement: Application Gateway subnets MUST include a delegation to `Microsoft.Network/applicationGateways` (required since 2025). AKS node subnets MAY include delegation to `Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters`. PostgreSQL Flexible Server subnets MUST include delegation to `Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL/flexibleServers`. Delegated subnets MUST be dedicated — no other resource types allowed in the same subnet.
rule.rationale: App Gateway v2 deploy fails without delegation as of 2025. Mixing non-delegated resources in a delegated subnet causes intermittent plan failures or silent drift on re-apply.

example.title: App Gateway subnet with delegation (Terraform)

section.name: hub-spoke-peering
section.p: Hub-spoke connectivity uses VNet peering. Peering is NOT transitive — spoke-to-spoke traffic requires a route through Azure Firewall in the hub with UDRs.

rule.statement: VNet peering MUST be created in BOTH directions (hub→spoke AND spoke→hub). A one-directional peering completes in the portal but traffic fails silently. Set `allow_forwarded_traffic = true` on both sides and `allow_gateway_transit = true` on the hub side when a VPN Gateway or ExpressRoute Gateway is present.
rule.rationale: Agents routinely create only one peering direction, the plan shows no errors, and connectivity fails. Both directions are required for the peering to be in Connected state.

example.title: Bidirectional peering (Terraform)
example.code:
resource "azurerm_virtual_network_peering" "hub_to_spoke" { name = "hub-to-${var.environment}" resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.hub.name virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.hub.name remote_virtual_network_id = azurerm_virtual_network.spoke.id allow_virtual_network_access = true allow_forwarded_traffic = true allow_gateway_transit = true }
resource "azurerm_virtual_network_peering" "spoke_to_hub" { name = "${var.environment}-to-hub" resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.spoke.name virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.spoke.name remote_virtual_network_id = azurerm_virtual_network.hub.id allow_virtual_network_access = true allow_forwarded_traffic = true use_remote_gateways = false }

example.title: One-directional peering
example.note: Missing spoke_to_hub peering. Connectivity appears fine in the Azure portal until first TCP attempt from spoke to hub fails.

section.name: hub-network-terraform
section.p: Complete hub network Terraform including optional Firewall and Bastion subnets.

example.title: Hub network with Bastion and optional Firewall (Terraform)

checklist.check: Address space planned — non-overlapping with on-premises or other VNets.
Subnet sizing calculated — reserved IPs for Azure services accounted for.
Hub-spoke topology confirmed for multi-app environments.
VNet peering created in BOTH directions.
DNS strategy defined (Azure DNS, custom DNS, hybrid).
Subnet delegations configured for managed services (App Gateway, AKS, PostgreSQL).
Bastion configured for management access (no public IPs on backend VMs).
UDR configured if spoke-to-spoke traffic must transit hub Firewall.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Network changes are blast-radius high. Have a planner agent produce a topology diff (current state to target state) including subnet CIDRs, peerings, route tables, and DNS zones. An implementation agent emits Terraform. A reviewer agent runs `terraform plan` and checks: no public IPs added without justification, peerings exist in both directions, no NSG rule is Any/Any/Allow.

rule.statement: Never run `az network vnet create` or `az network vnet peering create` ad-hoc. All VNet changes MUST go through `terraform plan` review before apply.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc changes create drift that breaks subsequent Terraform runs and produces stale state files.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Design an Azure hub-spoke network architecture for the following requirements:
Project: {project_name} Environments: {dev, staging, prod} Workloads: {AKS, App Service, PostgreSQL, Redis, Storage} Connectivity: {VPN to on-premises / ExpressRoute / Internet only} Compliance: {HIPAA, SOC2, PCI-DSS, none}
Requirements: - Address space planning with no overlaps - Subnet sizing for each tier - NSG rules following least privilege - Private endpoints for all PaaS services - NAT Gateway for outbound connectivity - Azure Bastion for secure access
Output: 1. IP address allocation table 2. Terraform code for VNet and subnets 3. Hub-spoke peering configuration 4. NSG rules matrix

Calculate subnet sizes for Azure VNet with these requirements:
Services: - AKS cluster: {node_count} nodes, {pods_per_node} pods per node - Application Gateway: {instance_count} instances - Azure Bastion: 1 instance - Private Endpoints: {pe_count} endpoints - VMs: {vm_count} virtual machines
Constraints: - Reserve 5 IPs per subnet (Azure reserved) - Allow 50% growth headroom - Keep subnet boundaries on /24, /25, /26, /27
Output: 1. Recommended CIDR blocks for each subnet 2. IP count breakdown 3. VNet total address space

section.name: ai-gotchas
section.p: Common LLM mistakes when generating VNet Terraform:

antipattern.description: Subscription scope confusion: `az network vnet peering` requires both directions; LLMs create one side, plan looks clean, traffic still fails.
antipattern.reason: One-directional peering silently completes but traffic is asymmetric. Always create both hub_to_spoke and spoke_to_hub resources.

antipattern.description: Agents propose a /28 or /27 subnet for AKS nodes, forgetting AKS reserves IPs for pods at the subnet level with Azure CNI.
antipattern.reason: AKS Azure CNI pre-allocates IPs for pods per node at node pool creation. A /27 (32 IPs minus 5 Azure reserved = 27) exhausts immediately with 2 nodes at 16 pods each.

antipattern.description: VNet peering does not transit through a third VNet by default; agents draw the hub-spoke diagram and forget UDR / Azure Firewall is needed for spoke-to-spoke traffic.
antipattern.reason: Azure does not route spoke-to-spoke via the hub without an explicit UDR pointing to the Firewall's private IP. Traffic is silently dropped.
